schools.summary: Henry: the name 'Jah' is stripped away in disgrace; Abijam was not like David, wanting sincerity, spared only for David's sake.
Ellicott: 'Abijah' is likely the true, more significant form; the variant may distinguish him from Jeroboam's son.
Gill fixes the chronology — the eighteenth year of Jeroboam proves Rehoboam reigned seventeen years.

sides.position: a deliberate disgrace, dropping the divine 'Jah'
likely a false reading or a mark to distinguish him from Jeroboam's son

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott: the heart not 'shalem' as David's; Maachah is the granddaughter, not daughter, of Absalom, harmonizing Kings and Chronicles.
Gill: the 'three years' are rounded — barely one full year and parts of two others.

debates.question: How is Maachah related to Abishalom (Absalom)?

sides.position: his granddaughter — 'daughter' used loosely for a descendant
the parallel names her Michaiah daughter of Uriel of Gibeah

schools.summary: Ellicott: the 'imperfect heart' means Abijam worshipped Jehovah only as chief of a pantheon — hence the irony of his championing the temple against Jeroboam.
Clarke: a heart not perfect is an idolatrous heart that will not support the worship of the true God.
Gill: the father's sins he walked in were chiefly idolatry, copied from both parents.
Keil stresses the 'because' — Abijam was spared only for David's sake; Ellicott grounds it in the covenant promise and the moral continuity of history.
Gill: the lamp is a glorious kingdom continued in David's posterity.
Clarke: the lamp is concretely 'a son to succeed him,' as promised in 1 Kings 11:36.

debates.question: What is the 'lamp' given for David's sake?

sides.position: a son and successor
the continued kingdom and light of David's house in Jerusalem

schools.summary: Clarke: the Uriah affair is David's one flagrant crime — 'a whole system of offenses' — yet repented and forgiven.
Ellicott: the qualifying clause, absent from the Vatican LXX, may be a marginal note or the compiler's comment.
Gill: David turned not aside deliberately, in worship or moral walk.
Henry: the blot remained on David's name, but repented sin did not cut off the covenant entail.

debates.question: Is 'save only in the matter of Uriah' original to the text?

sides.position: possibly a marginal note or compiler's comment, absent from the Vatican LXX
read as authentic and instructive of David's one great fault

schools.summary: Clarke argues 'Abijam' is the true reading (many MSS, Syriac, Arabic); Ellicott suspects textual error; Keil defends 'his life' as Abijam's.
Gill keeps 'Rehoboam' as head of the house, referring 'his life' to Abijam.

sides.position: yes — 'Abijam,' per many manuscripts and versions
text is probably corrupt, but the emendation is uncertain
keep 'Rehoboam'; 'his life' means Abijam's lifetime

schools.summary: Keil: Abijam reigned barely two years and a few months, the 'three years' being a rounded regnal reckoning.
Gill: the royal annals referred to seem to have been written by Iddo the prophet.
Gill: 'slept with his fathers' simply means he died as they did — the honoured burial covering an unworthy son.
Henry: God cut Abijam off early to make way for Asa, a better man; the covenant line descends peacefully.

themes: burial in the city of David

schools.summary: Henry: good kings' reigns were generally long; Asa's forty-one years and sincere (not sinless) heart open a time of reformation.
Gill fixes the synchronism — Asa began to reign in the twentieth year of Jeroboam.
Keil and Ellicott: Maachah, Asa's grandmother, kept the office of queen-mother (gebirah) into his reign, resolving the repeated name.
Clarke: 'daughter' is used loosely in Scripture for granddaughter or great-granddaughter.
Gill: Asa's long reign matched or exceeded David's and Solomon's — a blessing on a good king.
Keil details the acts implied here (purging idols, deposing Maacah); Ellicott makes the reign a turning-point, guided by prophets and sealed in covenant renewal.
Gill: Asa made David his pattern in personal walk, government, and above all religion.
Keil gathers the whole reforming sequence; Clarke notes the ironic title 'qedeshim,' the 'consecrated ones' of the high places.
Gill: Asa cleared the sodomites by exile or execution according to God's law.
Henry: reformation begins with removing evil, and much of it was found to do so soon after Solomon.
Clarke: the miphletseth was a Priapus-like obscene image; Maachah 'another Messalina,' deservedly deposed.
Ellicott: read 'for an asherah,' not 'in a grove'; the word means a monstrous, obscene abomination.
Gill: Maachah was removed from government or, more likely, from presiding over the idol's rites.
Henry: reformation must begin at home; Asa loved his grandmother, but loved God better.

sides.position: an obscene Priapus-like emblem of impure worship
an idol for the asherah, over whose rites she presided

schools.summary: Ellicott: the Chronicles show an attempted reform that failed; the craving for local sanctuaries defeated even earnest reformers.
Clarke: a thorough reformation was reserved for Jehoshaphat; the high places left were probably devoted to the true God.
Gill: the high places were old shrines of Jehovah's worship, hard and dangerous to remove.
Henry: a 'perfect' heart means sincerity, not sinlessness — the upright may still fall short of the good they could do.

debates.question: Were the un-removed high places a real fault?

sides.position: tolerable — devoted to the true God, hard to abolish
a genuine blemish the historian repeatedly laments

themes: a perfect heart is a sincere heart

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: the dedicated gold and silver were spoils of Asa's and Abijah's victories, replenishing the treasury Shishak had stripped.
Gill: Asa performed his father Abijam's unfulfilled vow of dedication.
Clarke: Asa held himself bound by his father's vow.
Henry: we must not only cease from evil but learn to do well, dedicating our all to God.
Keil and Ellicott: ten years' peace, the defeat of Zerah, and covenant renewal preceded open war; 'all their days' means the standing enmity.
Clarke: the 'thirty-sixth year' conflicts with Baasha's death in Asa's twenty-sixth — either reckon from the schism or grant a numerical error.
Gill (with Abarbanel): Baasha is named out of order so that Asa's failings are told before the rest of his history.

debates.question: How is the 'thirty-sixth year' of the war to be reconciled?

sides.position: count from the division of the kingdoms, not Asa's accession
there is a numerical error in the parallel record

schools.summary: Ellicott identifies Ramah (Er-Ram) as a menacing frontier fortress like Decelea; Keil notes Asa's move to bribe Benhadad.
Gill: the 'thirty-sixth year' is of the kingdom of Judah from the schism — i.e., Asa's sixteenth.
Clarke: Ramah, a hill commanding the road to Jerusalem, was fortified to cut off intercourse with Judah.
Ellicott: Asa rightly bribed Benhadad but sinfully went beyond it, calling in a heathen power against his brethren — hence prophetic rebuke.
Gill: temple treasures were not to be put to profane use, least of all for such a purpose.
Clarke identifies Benhadad as grandson of Rezon/Hezion, founder of the Damascus kingdom.
Henry: the league sprang from unbelief; carnal policy follows when a believer will not trust God fully.
Clarke: the alliance was needless — no danger Asa could not himself have met — and he robbed God's house to pursue Israel's ruin.
Gill: a standing treaty already existed, Benhadad's father having been a confederate of both kingdoms.
Keil: Benhadad consented and sent his captains against Israel's cities, forcing Baasha from Ramah.
Henry: to ask Benhadad to break his league was to tempt him to violate public faith — Asa's own sin.

themes: a human bond preferred to the covenant

schools.summary: Ellicott maps the smitten towns of upper Naphtali near the Jordan's source — the same tract later taken by Assyria.
Clarke: Benhadad struck the towns that formed the principal strength of the northern kingdom.
Gill: Benhadad broke his alliance and sent his standing army directly against Israel's cities.
Keil and Ellicott: Baasha withdrew to his capital Tirzah; Syria's new supremacy over Israel lasted till Ahab's time.
Clarke: Tirzah was the royal city, in which Baasha was now obliged to shut himself up.
Gill: Baasha heard what was being done in the north and abandoned Ramah.
Keil and Ellicott locate Geba (Jeba) and Mizpah (Scopus/Nebi Samwil), Asa's new northern outposts built from Ramah's spoil.
Clarke: a general levée en masse — in national danger all exemptions cease.
Gill: the proclamation summoned men of every rank and degree.
Keil and Ellicott: seeking physicians not the Lord, and imprisoning Hanani, show Asa's heart turning away as prosperity deteriorated his character.
Clarke: the gout began in his thirty-ninth year; he sought physicians rather than the Lord.
Gill: the acts of Asa are found partly in Chronicles, partly in the fuller royal annals.

debates.question: How should the feet-disease be read?

sides.position: as a chastening exposing self-reliance and decline
chiefly as a medical affliction of his old age

themes: decline in a good man's age

schools.summary: Gill: Asa was buried in state on a bed of spices in the sepulchre he had made in the city of David.
Clarke points to the Chronicler's record of Asa's splendid and costly funeral.
Keil: Jehoshaphat succeeded, and the narrative turns back to Israel and Nadab.
Henry: Israel, deserting God's house and David's, was 'a reed shaken in the water' — six or seven violent reigns while Asa reigned; Nadab kept the calves and was cut off.
Gill fixes the synchronism — Nadab began to reign in Asa's second year.

themes: a reed shaken in the water

schools.summary: Ellicott: the recurring formula means perseverance in Jeroboam's calf-worship; Keil traces the conspiracy of Baasha and the extermination of Jeroboam's house per Ahijah.
Gill: the evil is idolatry, than which nothing is greater in God's sight.

themes: the entail of a father's sin

schools.summary: Ellicott: Baasha, of obscure origin, is the first military usurper of Israel's throne — the ephemeral dynasties contrasting with David's covenanted line.
Clarke locates Gibbethon in Dan, generally held by the Philistines.
Gill: Baasha was probably an officer in Nadab's own army.
Gill: Baasha's sole or chief aim in the murder was to possess the kingdom — naked ambition.
Henry: Nadab's own army chose his murderer for successor; the motive was likeliest bare ambition.
Ellicott: Baasha acted from pure ambition, not a mission of vengeance — yet 'sin which works out God's purpose is not the less truly sin,' and he is later judged for it.
Clarke: the slaughter fulfilled Ahijah's prophecy — God used one wicked man to destroy another.
Gill: Baasha's aim was to leave no rival with a claim to the crown.
Henry: 'Herein he was barbarous, but God was righteous' — no word of God falls to the ground.

debates.question: Is Baasha excused as God's instrument of judgment?

sides.position: no — he acted from ambition and is himself judged for the blood he shed
God used a wicked man to fulfil Ahijah's prophecy against Jeroboam

schools.summary: Gill: the clause gives God's reason, not Baasha's motive — the Lord suffered the slaughter because of Jeroboam's provoking abominations.
Henry: Jeroboam's sin was punished and Ahijah's word accomplished — those who provoke God do it to their own confusion.
Gill: Nadab's acts were more than are related here, preserved in the fuller royal annals.
Henry (on the run of reigns): bad kings' reigns were short — Nadab's barely two years, busy but barren.

themes: separate registers for a divided kingdom
acts men record versus acts God weighs

schools.summary: Gill: the lifelong war sprang from irreconcilable allegiances — Asa to the temple, Baasha to the calves.
Keil: the verse simply repeats v.16, resuming the Asa-Baasha thread after the account of Nadab's fall.
Clarke refers back to his note on v.16 — one standing statement of continual hostility.
Keil: Baasha's reign is told briefly; Jehu's prophecy against his house echoes Ahijah's, and the kings' perversity is that they kept the very calves that had doomed the houses they overthrew.
Gill: the notice stresses that all Israel submitted to Baasha, usurper and murderer though he was.

themes: all Israel submits to a usurper
the same sin, the same judgment

schools.summary: Clarke: the entail of iniquity is cut only by thorough conversion; sin gathers strength by exercise and age.
Keil records the standing verdict — Baasha walked in Jeroboam's way and sin.
Gill notes the symmetry — Baasha did evil as Nadab did, whom he had slain.
Henry: Baasha rooted out the sinner but clave to the sin — malice and ambition, not zeal for God.
